text:
[]~EVIEWS 
Recent studies of the social amoeba Dictyostelium 
are contributing to our understanding of the role of 
cell-cell interactions in development. When deprived 
of nutrients, these cells cease growth and initiate a 
program in which 105 single amoebae form a multi- 
cellular organism. The aggregation of the cells is 
organized by extracellular adenosine 3', 5' cyclic phos- 
phate (cAMP) which mediates chemotaxis and oscil- 
latory cell-cell signaling (Fig. 1). The signaling derives 
from the capacity of each cell to synthesize and 
secrete cAMP in response to cAMP stimulation. Within 
aggregation centers, cAMP levels spontaneously oscil- 
late; each peak initiates a wave of cAMP that propa- 
gates through the cell monolayer. The leading edge of 
each passing cAMP wave provides a gradient that 
briefly 
orients 
the 
chemotactically 
sensitive 
cells 
towards the aggregation center. Cells move up the gra- 
dient for several minutes until the peak of the wave 
reaches the position of the cell. The cells then move 
randomly until the next wave elicits another coordinat- 
ed movement step. Within a few hours, these move- 
ments result in the formation of a multicellular struc- 
ture. During the following day, it undergoes a series of 
morphogenetic changes and a pattern forms along its 
axis. The cells differentiate into the two cell types,

Cell-cell interactions 
in Dictyostelium 
development 
PETER DEVREOTES 
The development of an organism requires extensive cell-cell 
communication; however, little is known about the signals 
transmitted among diffewntiattn& cells. Observations of 
DictyosteUum amoebae reveal that transmembrane 
signaling systems have been highly conserved in evolutio~ 
The signals that cause these cells to diffemtiae are 
processed by mechanisms similar to those that process 
sensory and hormonal stimuli in higher animals. 
A 
vacuolated nonviable stalks and durable spores, which 
constitute the mature fruiting body 1,2. 
Figure 2 is a schematic diagram of the cell--cell sig- 
naling system. High-affinity surface cAMP receptors 
mediate the chemotactic response as well as activation 
of adenylate cyclase. Secreted cAMP diffuses to nearby 
cells where it activates the same responses. It also

binds to the secreting cell, cre- 
ating a positive feedback loop. 
Following an initial discharge 
lasting several minutes, uncon- 
trolled production of cAMP is 
prevented by an adaptation 
mechanism 
that 
reversibly 
attenuates synthesis. An extra- 
cellular phosphodiesterase then 
lowers 
cAMP towards 
basal 
levels. 
This 
combination 
of 
opposing 
molecular 
forces 
makes the system excitable. 
This excitability accounts for 
the spontaneous cAMP oscilla- 
tions that occur at the aggrega- 
tion centers and also in syn- 
chronized suspensions of cells3. 
It has become clear that 
this cell-cell signaling system 
plays a central role in develop- 
ment, acting as a timer and 
controlling morphogenesis and 
pattern 
formation ~-9. 
Cyclic 
AMP, 
together 
with 
mor- 
phogens such as DIF (differen- 
tiation-inducing factor), regu- 
lates 
the 
classes 
of genes 
expressed 
at each stage of 
development and ensures that 
cells differentiate into prestalk 
and prespore cells in a pattern 
along the axis of the structure. 
This article will first describe 
the components of the signal- 
ing system, then discuss its role 
in gene expression, morpho- 
genesis and pattern formation.

FIGM 
Diagram of signal transduction pathways. Shown are 
interactions among receptor (R), guanine nucleotide-binding 
protein(s) (G), and adenylate cyclase (AC); frigid A mutations 
block all signal transduction events as shown; synag mutations 
specifically prevent cAMP signaling as shown. 
G-protein-linked signal transduction pathways 
Multiple G proteins 
The coupling of surface receptors to G proteins, a 
conserved family of guanine nucleotide-binding pro- 
teins, has emerged as a major signaling mechanism 
involved in sensory and hormone responsiveness. 
Perhaps the most significant lesson we are learning 
from studies of Dictyostelium is that G-protein-linked 
pathways are also involved in the regulation of gene 
expression via cell-cell interactions. The G proteins 
consist of heterotrimeric complexes that dissociate 
upon activation. The cz-subunit binds and hydrolyses 
GTP; the ~'y-subunits are required for recognition of 
the complex by the occupied receptor 10. Two homo- 
logues of the 0t-subunits, designated Gcd and G(x2, 
which are 45% identical with mammalian G proteins, 
have been identified 11, and one homologue of the 
~-subunit that is 63% identical to human J3 has also 
been found 12. Several standard assays of G-protein 
function have been applied in this system. First, in iso- 
lated membranes, the affinity of receptors for cAMP is 
lowered by GTP and GDp13. Second, adenylate cyclase 
is stimulated by GTP analogues TM. Mutations can inde- 
pendently affect each of these functions, suggesting 
that there are multiple classes of these signal-transduc- 
ing proteins (Fig. 2): frigid A mutations interfere with 
the regulation of cAMP-binding affinity; synag mu- 
tations 
prevent 
GTP 
stimulation 
of 
adenylate 
cyclase]4-16. 
These mutations are a subset of the approximately 
100 complementation groups that display aggregation- 
minus 
phenotypes: frigid 
A 
mutants 
have 
no 
detectable chemotactic or signaling responses and 
cannot be induced to differentiate; synag mutants have 
specifically lost the signaling response but can carry 
out chemotaxis and can differentiate if supplied with 
cAMP. Further studies of frigid A alleles showed that 
the Gee2 gene carries deletions or point mutations (Ref. 
17 and G. Pitt et al., in preparation) and that a wild- 
type phenotype can be restored to these mutants by 
transformation with a wild-type Ga2 gene (A. Kumagai 
et al., in preparation). Thus, G~2 is a key component 
of a major signal transduction pathway. As yet, the 
molecular defect in a synag mutant has not been 
defined. A soluble protein from wild-type cells that 
can restore GTP stimulation to the adenylate cyclase in 
the synag 7mutant is currently being purified TM. 
Surface cAMP receptors 
Figure 3A is a working model for the structure of 
the surface cAMP receptor 18. It contains seven putative 
membrane-spanning domains, the amino terminus is 
extracellular, 
and 
a 
serine-rich 
carboxy-terminal 
domain is intracellular. There are prolines and charges 
within the membrane-spanning domains that may con- 
tribute to cAMP binding. All these features are com- 
mon to the class of G-protein-linked receptors that

FIGll 
Time course and regulation of gene expression. Summary of RNA blot and 
nuclear run-on analysis of marker gene expression during development. The 
second line shows each of the classes of genes - growth, early, oscillation- 
induced, prestalk and prespore, and DIF-induced. The third line shows the 
developmental stage at which each class is expressed. The signals to which 
the gene classes respond, indicated by the arrows at the top, are produced 
by the cells during normal development. When cells are prevented from 
aggregating these cell--cell interactions do not occur and development is 
blocked. However, expression of all of the gene classes will occur if the 
signals are applied exogenously. 
includes rhodopsin, the 0t- and 13-adrenergic receptors, 
the serotonin receptor, and the muscarinic acetyl- 
choline receptor. 
On SDS-polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis, the 
cAMP receptor consists of a doublet of bands (molecu- 
lar weights 40 000 and 43 000) that display a striking 
feature19: their relative intensity oscillates in synchrony 
with the spontaneous oscillations in cAMP signaling 
~Fig. 3B). When the oscillations are blocked, cells 
gradually attain a basal state and receptors are found 
predominantly as the smaller form; exogenous cAMP 
stimulates a rapid transition to the larger form 20. This 
transition is accompanied by a dramatic increase in 
phosphorylation. Four to six serines, contained in the 
carboxy-terminal domain, are modified within a few 
minutes21,22. 
The adaptation properties of several rapid cellular 
responses such as myosin phosphorylation and adeny- 
late cyclase activation have been carefully exam- 
ined 2~-27. Several major properties of adaptation have 
emerged from these studies. Cells respond to increases 
in the fractional occupancy of receptors. When occu- 
pancy is held constant, responses subside within a few 
minutes. The magnitude of a response is proportional 
to the increment in occupancy. When the stimulus is 
withdrawn, adaptation decays with a half-life of sever- 
al minutes and cells regain sensitivity in about 15 min- 
utes. The pattern of modulation of phosphorylation is 
strongly correlated with the adaptation process 2a,a3. As 
the level of phosphorylation approaches a new steady 
state, the physiological responses subside. When the 
stimulus is withdrawn, the cells regain sensitivity at the 
same rate as the decay in phosphorylation. Similar cor- 
relations of phosphorylation with desensitization are 
observed for rhodopsin and the 13-adrenergic receptor. 
Thus, studies of adaptation mechanisms in this simple 
organism may apply directly to the G-protein-linked 
receptors in higher eukaryotic organisms. 
Strong evidence that the surface cAMP receptor 
plays an essential role in morphogenesis and develop- 
ment has been obtained from observations of cell lines 
that fail to express the protein. These lines can be con- 
structed by transformation with a vector that expresses 
antisense RNA (Ref. 18 and T. Sun et al., in prepara- 
tion). During starvation, the control cells aggregate and 
form streams in parallel with wild-type 
cells; the antisense transformants remain as 
a cell monolayer, without aggregating. The 
cAMP receptor protein is undetectable by 
immunoblot analysis. The cells display only 
very low levels of cAMP-binding sites 
(0.5%) and weak chemotactic and cAMP 
signaling responses. Few developmentally 
regulated genes are expressed. Thus the 
surface cAMP receptors are required for 
aggregation 
and 
for cells to 
progress 
through the developmental program. 
Gene expression 
Figure 4 illustrates a simplified classifi- 
cation of the genes expressed during devel- 
opmentS-9,28, 29. One 
class of genes 
is 
induced by starvation alone. Other classes 
require repeated application of cAMP for 
induction 
or 
are 
repressed 
by 
this 
treatment. 
Expression of the cell-type-specific genes, characteris- 
tic of prestalk or prespore cells, requires intermittent 
followed by continuous stimulation by cAMP. Finally, 
expression of a class of stalk genes requires DIF in 
addition to cAMP. There is an intriguing feature of the 
regulation by intermittent application of cAMP: low, 
constant concentrations of exogenous cAMP profound- 
ly inhibit these genes and early development, while 
repeated application of cAMP at six-minute intervals, 
which mimics the endogenous oscillations, accelerates 
these processes. This suggests that the receptor- 
mediated responses that activate gene expression dis- 
play adaptation and that the cAMP oscillator regulated 
by receptor-mediated phosphorylation (and dephos- 
phorylation) may be an essential part of the develop- 
mental timer. In contrast, once the cells are made 
competent by repeated stimulation, the late cell-type- 
specific gene expression depends 
on continuous 
receptor occupancy. Thus, the responses that activate 
the late cell-type-specific genes must not adapt (see 
Fig. 4). 
Morphogenesis and pattern formation 
As the multicellular structure forms, a simple pat- 
tern appears along its axis. Cells within the posterior 
three-quarters of its length express 'prespore' markers 
and can be distinguished from those at the anterior, 
which do not express these markers. Since the anterior 
cells later differentiate into stalk cells, they are desig- 
nated prestalk cells. The ratio of cell types can be 
altered pharmacologically and by mutation30. 
This pattern arises from both chemotactic cell sort- 
ing and through subsequent position-dependent differ- 
entiation. Cells that have undergone periods of starva- 
tion, that enter the developmental program from early 
stages of the cell cycle, or that have been grown on 
glucose-deficient medium preferentially sort to the 
anterior region. Subsequent prespore marker accumu- 
lation is confined to the posterior region of the slug, 
and, later, stalk cell differentiation takes place at the 
tip and ventral side of the structure, indicating that 
there are position-dependent developmental cues 4. 
The tip of the slug displays properties of an embry- 
onic 'organizer'. If a slug is severed just behind the tip, 
TIG AUGUST 1989 VOL 5, NO. 8 
[]~EVIEWS 
the tissue connected to the tip continues to migrate. 
However, migration of the remainder ceases, the cell 
mass reforms a mound, and a new tip appears before 
morphogenesis continues. Excised tips, grafted to host 
slugs, define new axes and thereby cause several 
smaller slugs to form and separate. These organiza- 
tional properties of the tip appear to derive from its 
capacity to generate oscillatory cAMP signaling31,32. 
The roles of adenosine and differentiation-inducing 
factor (DIF) in the multicellular structure are being 
explored. Adenosine appears to antagonize the actions 
of extracellular cAMP, and its production in anterior 
regions has been postulated to inhibit the formation of 
additional tips and to prevent anterior cells from 
expressing prespore genes33. DIF has been identified 
as 
1-(3,5-dichloro-2,6-dihydroxy-4-methoxy-phenyl)- 1- 
hexanone, a lipid-like compound that is essential for 
stalk 
cell 
differentiation. 
Although 
certain 
marker 
mRNAs have been shown to be dependent on DIF for 
expression and to be expressed only in anterior cells, 
DIF itself is found in higher levels in the posterior cells. 
Mutations that affect DIF production shift the proportion 
of cell types within the multicellular structure. 
Conclusions 
The mechanisms of signal transduction in eukary- 
otic microorganisms appear to be similar to those in 
mammalian cells, and thus these simple creatures pro- 
vide convenient models for study of relevant signal 
transduction 
phenomena. 
The 
characterization 
of 
mutants such as frigid A and the synags and cAMP 
receptor antisense cell lines has defined a major signal 
transduction pathway. These studies have established 
that G-protein-linked signaling systems play a key role 
in the developmental program. 
It has 
become 
clear 
that 
signal 
transduction 
through surface cAMP receptors participates in a multi- 
tude of developmental functions. Can a single surface 
receptor carry out so many functions? Our preliminary 
evidence suggests that there is a family of related sur- 
face cAMP receptor subtypes and that each subtype 
may be responsible for one or a few of the cAMP- 
mediated functions. We must suppose that expression 
of antisense RNA of the first subtype blocks expression 
of all the receptor subtypes, since the antisense cell 
lines are defective in most aspects of the developmen- 
tal program. 
Many of the events associated with signaling were 
first inferred from observations of cell monolayers 
which can form elegant ring and spiral patterns resem- 
bling the patterns produced in the Zabotinsky oscilla° 
tory chemical reaction (Fig. 1A). Embryologists should 
note, however, that these patterns are apparent only 
under favorable conditions. Frequently the aggregates 
resemble embryonic tissue in which the motions of the 
individual cells are difficult to trace. If similar signaling 
systems exist in higher animals, they may be difficult 
to detect. 
There 
are 
major 
apparent 
differences 
between 
Dictyostelium development 
and 
that 
of 
embryos. In Dictyostelium, a cell mass forms from the 
aggregation of a population of independent cells; in 
an embryo, a highly ordered cell mass forms from the 
division of a single cell. Whether the roles for the 
steps of morphogenesis and pattern formation that 
follow, and appear similar in each case, are more fun- 
damental than these differences will determine the 
future usefulness of Dictyostelium as a model for 
development.
